text: The surgeon uses an OptiView in the left upper quadrant, revealing extensive omental adhesions and a large piece of iPOM mesh covering the midline. A higher left upper quadrant port is established for camera access. The surgeon employs a ligature to carefully dissect and remove adhesions, staying close to the abdominal wall and mesh to avoid leaving dead fat and prevent bleeding due to the patient's cirrhosis. The falciform ligament is visible, not previously taken down during ventral hernia repair. The surgical field is cleared, aiming towards the liver, and the robot is docked for further adhesiolysis with a super pubic port.

The surgeon uses an OptiView technique in the left upper quadrant, revealing extensive omental adhesions and a large iPOM mesh covering the midline. A higher left upper quadrant port is utilized for camera access. The surgeon employs a ligature to carefully dissect and remove the adhesions, staying close to the abdominal wall and mesh to avoid leaving dead fat and prevent bleeding, especially considering the patient's cirrhosis.

The surgeon has cleared enough space to expose the falciform ligament, which was not taken down during the previous ventral hernia repair. Additional adhesions are being removed to create more working room, with the view directed towards the liver. The robotic system is being docked, with arm number two positioned super pubically. Further adhesiolysis is performed, addressing adhesions from a prior open liver resection. Visible tattoos near the hepatic flexure suggest previous endoscopic procedures.

The surgeon navigates through adhesions resulting from a prior open liver resection, revealing a tattoo near the hepatic flexure. The transverse colon is plastered to the anterior abdominal wall, and the surgeon works to separate it from the falciform ligament. The superior extent of the mesh is visible, and the surgeon attempts to create space between the colon and the stomach, exposing the posterior aspect of the stomach.

The surgeon navigates between the transverse colon and the falciform ligament, releasing the transverse colon adhered to the anterior abdominal wall. The superior extent of the mesh is visible. The surgeon attempts to create space between the colon and the stomach, revealing the back of the stomach and a fused lesser sac. The hepatic flexure is plastered to the liver edge. The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection at the ileocolic pedicle, thinning out the pedicle to expose the artery and vein. Fibrotic tissues are noted, common in cirrhosis patients. The surgeon marks the mesentery for transverse colon mesenteric transection, dissecting over the right kidney and noting fibrotic tissue and tattoos in the retroperitoneum. The transverse colon resection line is marked, with the middle colics visible, and the surgeon plans the distal margin before attempting to access the lesser sac around the flexure.

The surgeon navigates the fused lesser sac, likely due to previous surgeries, and begins dissection at the iliocolic pedicle. The hepatic flexure is plastered to the liver edge, and the surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, thinning the pedicle near the superior mesenteric vessels to expose the artery and vein. The ileocolic artery is taken separately, amidst fibrotic tissue typical in cirrhotic patients. The duodenum and tattooed retroperitoneum are visible. The surgeon marks the mesentery for transverse colon transection, dissecting over the right kidney and Girota's fascia, and plans the distal margin for resection.

The surgeon places a sponge to manage minor bleeding and proceeds to dissect the hepatic flexure, which is adhered to the bed of the previous liver resection and cholecystectomy. The thick tissue is dissected in layers using a ligature device, facilitating deeper access below the surface of the liver. The absence of a gallbladder and roux reconstruction is noted, confirming the liver's position on the opposite side of the colon.

The surgeon places a sponge to manage minor bleeding and proceeds to dissect the hepatic flexure, which is adhered to the bed of a previous liver resection and cholecystectomy. The thick tissue is dissected in layers using a ligature device for precision. The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure, reaching deeper layers near the liver surface, and continues the dissection towards the medial aspect over the duodenum. The terminal ileum is mobilized from an inferior trajectory to connect with the transverse colon for anastomosis, ensuring the retroperitoneal plane is accessed for efficient dissection.

The surgeon reaches the medial dissection over the duodenum, removing the residual tissue with a vessel sealer. The hepatic flexure is taken down, revealing the kidney. The terminal ileum is mobilized from an inferior trajectory to connect with the transverse colon for anastomosis, navigating through scarred tissue from a previous appendectomy.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the colon, freeing it from surrounding tissues. The small bowel mesentery is thicker than usual, requiring the use of bipolar cautery for effective sealing. The mesentery is divided in leaflets due to its thickness. The transverse colon mesentery is finished up to the predetermined margin, marked by a tattoo approximately five centimeters proximal to the polyp. The surgeon checks perfusion and prepares for anastomosis, opting to staple from the left upper quadrant port for optimal positioning.

The surgeon completes the division of the small bowel mesentery, which is thicker than usual, using bipolar cautery after the vessel sealer fails. The transverse colon mesentery is finished up to the marked margin, with a tattoo indicating the proximal extent of the polyp. The surgeon checks perfusion and staples the anastomosis from the left upper quadrant port, aligning the small bowel to the colon in an isoparastaltic fashion. A colotomy and enterotomy are made, and the stapler is inserted. The surgeon uses a vascular load to minimize bleeding and sutures the anastomosis closed with a V-lock suture, taking larger bites and performing a Lembert stitch for reinforcement. The robotic trocars are closed with Prolene sutures, and the specimen is exteriorized below the mesh level.

The surgeon divides the small bowel and positions the specimen above the liver. The small bowel is aligned with the colon in an isoparastaltic fashion, followed by the creation of a colotomy and enterotomy. A stapler is inserted with a vascular load to minimize bleeding, and the absence of bleeding is confirmed visually. The surgeon sutures the incision closed using a nine-inch V-lock suture, starting at the near corner and sewing around the incision. Larger bites are taken to ensure closure, and the suture is locked and cut after covering the incision with a second layer. The trocars are closed with a proline suture, and the mesh is exteriorized through a suprapubic incision.
